revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:花笠]]
{{Unreferenced|date=December 2016}} {{Otheruses|decorated kasa|a type of cherry blossom|Hanagasa}} [[File:Hanagasa Festa 2002.jpg|thumb|right|Hanagasa from the Yamagata Hanagasa Festival]] [[File:Danses d’Okinawa (musée Guimet, Paris) (11160081943).jpg|thumb|right|upright|Hanagasa used in Ryukyuan dance]]
A '''Hanagasa''' (花笠) is a decorated {{ill|kasa|en|kasa|qid=Q525242|lt=kasa|1=Kasa}} (traditional Japanese hat) or {{ill|Umbrella|en|Umbrella|qid=Q41607|lt=Umbrella|1=Umbrella}}. The hanagasa is widely used across Japan as part of costumes in traditional performing arts and festival dances.
In rituals and traditional performances where the dancer serves as the {{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}} (vessel for the [[kami]]), the performer may wear masks, heavy makeup, or full-head coverings (such as in {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|qid=Q713866|lt=Lion dance|1=Lion dance}}) to distinguish themselves from everyday life ("[[hare and ke|ke]]"). Similarly, wearing a kasa that obscures the face can symbolize the performer becoming a vessel for the divine. When such kasa are adorned, they are called hanagasa.
In [[Goryō|goryō shinkō]] (ancestral spirit worship), kasa and umbrellas are regarded as objects closest to {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}} and may serve as resting places for {{ill|Yakubyōgami|ja|疫病神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakubyōgami|1=Yakubyōgami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578909}} (disease spirits). Hanagasa are sometimes treated as yorishiro themselves, with villagers dancing in circles around them—similar to [[:en:Bon odori|Bon odori]]—to attract malevolent spirits. The hanagasa, carrying these spirits, would then be burned or cast into the sea to exorcise evil spirits. For example, in the [[Yasurai Festival]], flower-laden umbrellas called "hanagasa" are carried, and the flowers are thought to absorb malevolent spirits, which wither along with the flowers.
The decoration of kasa (or umbrellas) varies widely. It may involve attaching flower baskets with fresh flowers to the center of the kasa, decorating the entire kasa with [[:en:artificial flowers|artificial flowers]], or affixing artificial flowers to bamboo strips hanging from the kasa's edge. In the case of the Ryukyuan hanagasa, the kasa itself is crafted in the shape of a flower.
A cultivar of [[Prunus serrulata]] known as [[Hanagasa]] was named after its resemblance to the hanagasa's floral design.
== See Also == * [[Hanagasa Odori]] * [[Hanagasa Matsuri]]
{{Japan-culture-stub}}
